coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and biological applications of luminescent polyamidoamine dendrimers with aggregation-induced emission feature
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Qiulan
Liu, Meiying
Wang, Ke
Mao, Liucheng
Xu, Dazhuang
Zeng, Guangjian
Liang, Shangdong
Deng, Fengjie
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: The luminescent polymeric nanoparticles (LPNs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties have emerged as one of the most promising nanoprobes for their unique optical properties. The typical optical feature of AIE-active LPNs should be their obviously enhanced luminescence in aggregation or solid state, which can effectively overcome aggregation caused quenching effects of conventional organic dyes. In this study, we reported for the first time that highly emissive LPNs (named as TPE-E-PAMAM) can be simply fabricated via direct conjugation of tetraphenylethene derivative (TPE-E) with polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimers, which relied on a one-step Michael addition reaction between ene group of TPE-E and amino group of PAMAM dendrimers under rather mild experimental conditions. Because of the strong intermolecular interaction of TPE-E PAMAM copolymers, they can form compact spheres in water and exhibit strong fluorescence in aqueous solution. TPE-E PAMAM LPNs possess high water dispersity, uniform morphology and desirable biocompatibility for biological imaging. More importantly, with large numbers of amino groups on the shell and valid space in the core, PAMAM-TPE-E LPNs have great potential for targeted gene delivery. Taken together, we described a facile one-step covalent strategy for developing AIE-active amphipathic dendrimers, which showed great potential for biomedicine applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
Branched luminescent polymers
Biological imaging
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity of TPE-E-PAMAM NPs and cellular imaging

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of TPE-E and TPE-E-PAMAM. A number of characteristic peaks at 3276 and 2929 cm−1 was found in the sample of TPE-E-PAMAM. While the adsorption peak at 966 cm−1 disappeared in TPE-E-PAMAM. The difference implied that TPE-E had truly conjugated with PAMAM through Michael addition reaction.

(A) 1H NMR spectrum of TPE-E; (B) 1H NMR spectrum of TPE-E of TPE-E-PAMAM using D2O as the solvent. Inset of Fig. 2B is the 1H NMR spectrum of TPE-E-PAMAM using deuterated dichloromethane as the solvent.

(A) TEM image of TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs dispersed in water, scale bar =200nm; (B) UV–vis spectrum of TPE-E-PAMAM. The insets of Fig. 3B are the photographs of TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs in water under natural light (left cuvette) and UV light at 365nm (right cuvette).
(A) Fluorescence excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra of TPE-E-PAMAM (λ EM =452nm, λ EX =263–333nm); (B) Fluorescence emission (EM) spectra of TPE-E-PAMAM after irradiated by UV lamp for 1h
Cell viability of TPE-E-PAMAM to HeLa cells. Cells were incubated with 10–120µg/mL of TPE-E-PAMAM for 8 and 24h. The cell viability was determined using MTS assay.

CLSM images of TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs using a 405nm laser. (A) Fluorescent image, (B) bright fields, (C) merged image. Cells were incubated with 40µg/mL of TPE-E-PAMAM for 3h. It can be seen that the fluorescent signals were mainly distributed in the location of cytoplasm but not cell nucleus. Scale bar=20µm.
Reaction scheme for the synthesis of PAMAM-TPE-E through one-step reaction between the ene bond of TPE-E and amino groups of PAMAM-dendrimers.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and biological applications of luminescent polyamidoamine dendrimers with aggregation-induced emission feature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physiology, Medical School of Nanchang University, Nanchang 330006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao 266003, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The luminescent polymeric nanoparticles (LPNs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties have emerged as one of the most promising nanoprobes for their unique optical properties. The typical optical feature of AIE-active LPNs should be their obviously enhanced luminescence in aggregation or solid state, which can effectively overcome aggregation caused quenching effects of conventional organic dyes. In this study, we reported for the first time that highly emissive LPNs (named as TPE-E-PAMAM) can be simply fabricated via direct conjugation of tetraphenylethene derivative (TPE-E) with polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimers, which relied on a one-step Michael addition reaction between ene group of TPE-E and amino group of PAMAM dendrimers under rather mild experimental conditions. Because of the strong intermolecular interaction of TPE-E PAMAM copolymers, they can form compact spheres in water and exhibit strong fluorescence in aqueous solution. TPE-E PAMAM LPNs possess high water dispersity, uniform morphology and desirable biocompatibility for biological imaging. More importantly, with large numbers of amino groups on the shell and valid space in the core, PAMAM-TPE-E LPNs have great potential for targeted gene delivery. Taken together, we described a facile one-step covalent strategy for developing AIE-active amphipathic dendrimers, which showed great potential for biomedicine applications.

Aggregation induced emission (AIE) active branched polymeric nanoparticles were fabricated via a facile one-step conjugation reaction between the amino groups of polyamidoamine dendrimers and ene group of AIE dye.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence nanostructures have been widely used in biological studies due to its high sensitivity, diversity and non-destructive features, which can real-time, visualize the physiological alteration and monitor biological processes in living organisms. Numerous of fluorescence nanoparticles with different compositions and optical properties have been developed and widely applied in biological imaging, molecular diagnosis and intracellular gene/drug delivery [1-7]. Especially the luminescent nanoparticles polymeric (LPNs) with strong fluorescent intensity, low cytotoxicity and biodegradable potential have attracted the attention in recent years [8-13]. Self-assembly of amphiphilic luminescent copolymers is the commonly adopted method for fabrication of these LPNs. However, the LPNs based on conventional organic dyes often suffer from the notorious aggregation-caused quenching effect (ACQ), which was caused by π–π stacking of hydrophobic dyes during the self-assembly procedure [14-17]. In 2001, Tang and co-workers reported the abnormal fluorescence emission phenomenon that showed that the organic dye (1-methyl-1, 2, 3, 4, 5-pentaphenylsilole) could emit much strong luminescence in its solid state. This phenomenon was named as aggregation induced emission (AIE), which provided an elegant route to fabricate LPNs with strong luminescence due to the unique optical feature of AIE-active dyes [18]. With the mechanism that restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) suppresses the nonradiative pathway and switches on the radiative channel, a large numbers of AIE-active dyes have been synthesized and explored for biosensing and imaging applications [19-28]. Several methods have been employed to modify AIE-active dyes with recognition elements and hydrophilic groups to endow them specific affinity and water dispersion. Liu's group has conjugated both hydrophilic linker and targeting ligand with TPE derivates for specific cell thiol imaging via the coupling reaction [29]. Zhang and other researchers have also developed a number of water dispersion AIE-active LPNs through non-covalent self-assembly, covalent linkage, emulsion polymerization and RAFT polymerization, formation of dynamic bonds and so on [30-34]. However, developing an effective and simple method to fabricate water dispersible AIE-active LPNs with large numbers of functional groups and specific molecular structure is still desirable.

Polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimers, as highly branched nanostructured macromolecules with monodispersed mass, multifunctional surface and encapsulated core, have been keeping them in the spotlight [35-39]. Because of the unique tree-like branching architecture that originates from an initiator core as concentric, symmetrical monomer branching shells, PAMAM dendrimers have been widely used in various fields, such as drug delivery, catalysis supports, DNA or siRNA delivery and MRI contrast agents [22,40-52]. PAMAM dendrimers can form into spherical structures in solution through the electrostatic interaction of ammonium-carboxylate ion pairs. They can cross cell barriers by both paracellular and transcellular pathways. So, drugs can be attached to dendrimer terminal groups via covalent linker or encapsulated in void spacers through the electrostatic interaction. Meanwhile, targeting moieties can be designed to be conjugated to the surfaces, which can deliver drugs to targeted disease sites effectively. All these characteristics make PAMAM dendrimers ideal delivery vectors. Compared to linear polymers, dendrimers can form a tightly packed ball in water with lower viscosity. Unlike conventional micelles, dendrimers do not have a critical micelle concentration (CMC) because of the unimolecular nature [53]. So, the micelles still maintained thermodynamically stable when they are injected into the bloodstream and subjected to severe dilution, which can prevent the burst-release of entrapped drugs. Since both AIEgens and PAMAM dendrimers have been the focus of many research and have broaden future in biomedicine application, bringing them together can open a new breakthrough not only in imaging application but also in studying drug release mechanism. The small internal cavities can restrict the intramolecular rotation of AIE chromophores and boost fluorescence. Meanwhile, the numerous amino groups on the shell can make it soluble in physiological solution. However, only one study has demonstrated the fabrication of AIE-active PAMAM dendrimers through non-covalent interaction [25].

In this work, a facile one-step strategy was developed to fabricate AIEgens based PAMAM dendrimers. Tetraphenyl ethylene derivatives (TPE-E) with ene bond was conjugated with PAMAM dendrimers (G =3.0) through Michael addition reaction, which can occur under rather mild experimental conditions such as air atmosphere, low temperature, absent of metal catalysts in benign solvents. The amphiphilic AIE-active nanoparticles (TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs) not only showed high water-solubility due to the presence of PAMAM dendrimers but also exhibited strong fluorescence for the AIE properties of TPE-E. The nanoparticles have been applied into HeLa cells and showed strong fluorescence and high cell uptake efficiency. With the unique characteristics of both PAMAM dendrimers and TPE-E, the TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs have the potential to be widely used in biomedicine in future.

Bromotriphenylethylene, tetrakis palladiumbrt (triphenylphosphine), tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB), 4-Carboxy-benzeneboronic acid, methyl alcohol, and acryloyl chloride (99%) purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) were used as received. PAMAM dendrimers (MW: 6909Da, Gn=3.0) were pursed from chemical new material company of WeiHai Chengyuan. 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxy-methoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolim assays (MTS; Promega, USA), anhydrous tetrahydrofuran (THF, J&K Chemical), ethyl acetate (J&K Chemical) and triethylamine (Et3N, J&K Chemical, 99.5%) were used without further purification. Penicillin–streptomycin, fetal bovine serum (FBS) and trypsin-EDTA solution were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance-400 spectrometer with D2O or CDCl3 as solvents and tetramethylsilane (TMS) as internal standard. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a PELS-55 spectrometer. The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by a Perkin Elmer LAMBDA 35 UV/Vis system. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Nicolet 380 Fourier transform spectrometer with a resolution of 2 cm−1. The size and morphology of samples was carried out using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) by drop of a drop particle ethanol suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid.

The schematic diagram illustrating the synthesis route was presented in Scheme 1 . The reaction intermediates tetraphenyl ethylene-acrylate (TPE-E) was carried out according to the previously described method [54]. Briefly, bromotriphenylethylene (1.675g, 10mmol) and 4-carboxybenzene boronic acid (2.25g, 15mmol) were dissolved in the 60mL THF, then, 0.32g TBAB and 2M potassium carbonate aqueous solution (18mL) were added in the solution. After the mixture was stirred at 55 °C under nitrogen for about 0.5h, Pd(PPh3)4 (0.010g, 8.70 × 10−3 mmol) was added in the mixture and then heated to 90 °C for 24h, after purified, the product(TPE-O) was obtained. 417.6mg TPE-O (1.2mmol) and 350mg acryloyl chloride (3.6mmol) was mixed in 10mL absolute THF, and then triethylamine (1mL) was added dropwise. The reaction was stirred in ice-bath under nitrogen until TPE-O completely reacted. The product TPE-E was acquired after dried under vacuum.

Unlike oil-in-water (O/W) single emulsion solvent evaporation method, the TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs were synthesized by one-step Michael addition reaction through the reaction of ene bond and amino group. TPE-E (50mg) was dissolved in 6mL ethyl acetate and PAMAM (270mg) was dissolved in 2mL methyl alcohol, then the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 8h (monitored by TLC until no TPE-E was rest). Then, the polymer solution was washed with water and extracted with ethyl acetate. After dried through anhydrous Na2SO4, organic solvents were removed on a rotary evaporator at 50 °C under vacuum to obtain pure TPE-E-PAMAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of TPE-E-PAMAM NPs and cellular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of TPE-E-PAMAM NPs against HeLa cells was assessed by using the MTS assay. The cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal calf serum, penicillin/streptomycin, and l-glutamine at 37 °C with 5% CO2 with a density of 4×104 cells/well for 24h. Then, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing various concentrations of TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs (0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 120µg/mL) and then were incubated for 8 and 24h, respectively. MTS assay was performed as a standard of cytotoxicity of TPE-E-PAMAM by measuring absorbance at 570nm. The experiment was performed in triplicate. Cell viability rate was calculated as percentage compared to the control cells: (absorbance of the treated wells)/(absorbance of the control wells)×100%.

After cells were cultured in DMEM for 24h, 200µL TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs (40µg/mL) was added and the cells were incubated for 3h at 37 °C. The uptake behavior of TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs was examined using a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) Zeiss 710 3-channel (Zeiss, Germany) at 405nm excitation wavelength after fixed by 75% ethanol for 10min.

The Michael addition reaction method was successfully employed to fabricate AIEgens based nanoparticles. PAMAM was selected as the hydrophilic macromolecule due to its unique structure, good solubility and large amount of amino groups at their periphery, which can be protonated and interact with organic counterions to form stable supramolecular aggregates. More importantly, the active amino groups at the periphery can easily connect with target substances or be further modified. The TPE derivative, TPE-E, was synthesized and used as the AIEgen, which has shown a prosperous development in recent years and been widely used in many research fields [55-57]. As shown in Scheme 1, after TPE-E was synthesized, the ene bond of TPE-E was conjugated to the terminal amino moiety of PAMAM through the Michael addition reaction to form the hyperbranched amphiphilic TPE-E-PAMAM copolymers. The mass of final product was about 219.5mg, and the ratio of TPE-E and PAMAM was about 4.5:1. The formation of TPE-E-PAMAM was confirmed by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1 ). As exhibited in the FT-IR spectrum of TPE-E, a series of peaks at 1240–1600 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of benzyls. The absorptions at 966 cm−1 and 1732 cm−1 were also found, which indicated the presence of double bond (CC-) and ester carbonyl. Compared with TPE-E, the wide and strong absorption band at 3276 cm−1 and other small absorption peaks (3064 cm−1, 2929 cm−1) appeared in the sample of TPE-E-PAMAM, while the stretching vibration of double bond disappeared. The above comparisons suggested that ene bond of TPE-E was successfully conjugated with the amino groups of PAMAM dendrimers through Michael addition reaction. Moreover, the new vibration bands at 1357 cm−1 identified as CN stretch vibration occurred in TPE-E-PAMAM, suggesting the original double bond was broken and TPE-E was connected to PAMAM dendrimers successfully.

The structure of TPE-E-PAMAM was further verified by the 1H NMR spectra. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the signals assignable to the protons of benzyl at 7.39–7.02ppm and 5.84ppm ascribed to the protons of double bond were identified in TPE-E sample. We found an interesting phenomenon from 1H NMR spectra of TPE-E-PAMAM. When TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs were fully dissolved in deuterium oxide (D2O), they exhibited typical protons of PAMAM dendrimers. Such as the signals from 2.29 to 2.67ppm and 3.12ppm due to the peripheral ethylene of PAMAM dendrimers Ha and Hb proton (showed in Fig. 2B). However, the vibration of benzene proton was not observed in spectrum of TPE-E-PAMAM. While using deuterated dichloromethane as solvent, the symbol peaks belong to the protons of benzene from 6.99 to 7.21ppm were detected (inserted picture of Fig. 2B). Only a small vibration proton peaks assignable to PAMAM dendrimers were observed. Besides, the representative peaks of double bond were disappeared; further suggesting that TPE-E reacted with PAMAM dendrimers through Michael addition reaction. This phenomenon can be attributed to the core/shell structure of micelles. When in D2O, the hydrophilic PAMAM dendrimers were extended to the surface and formed into tight spherical shell. The hydrophobic segment, TPE-E, was buried inside the micelle and not exposed to aqueous D2O environment, which result in the shielding of the benzene peaks. However, in deuterated dichloromethane environment, the hydrophobic segment was exposed to the organic solvent while only part of PAMAM dendrimers were dissolved in the solvent. The same circumstance was also reported by Tae et al. [58]. Different from other hydrophilic macromolecules, PAMAM dendrimers were highly hyperbranched and can self-assemble into tightly core-shell structure in water. In a word, the FT-IR and 1H NMR spectra confirmed the formation of TPE-E-PAMAM successful.

Since smaller particle sizes (<200nm) not only can reduce the level of reticuloendothelial system (RES) uptake, but also can maintain minimal renal excretion and provide passive tumor targeting ability via the enhanced permeability and retention effect, so stability and size of the micelles are important for drug delivery [59]. The size and morphology of the TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs were estimated by TEM. PAMAM dendrimers terminated in hydrophilic groups were soluble in water while TPE-E was hydrophobic. So, the nanoparticles were formed with hydrophilic amino group as the outer shell and TPE-E as core through self-assembly. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the diameter of the aggregates was in the range of 50–200nm with spherical shape. The different size of these nanoparticles may be ascribed to the aggregation of unimolecular micelles and multimolecular micelles through intramolecular and intermolecular interactions, respectively. The mechanism for formation of unimolecular and multimolecular micelles may attribute to the unique structure of TPE-E–PAMAM. The unimolecule PAMAM dendrimers can self-assemble into compact globe in water because of the hyperbranched property, and part of the unimolecular micelles of TPE-E–PAMAM LPNs can aggregate into approximate spherical large multimolecular micelles owing to the presence of large number of amino groups at periphery, which was also called secondary aggregation. The formation of secondary aggregation was also reported by other researchers [60-63]. The stability of the micelles was evaluated by observing the morphology change of TPE-E–PAMAM with the micelles stored at 4 °C in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution for 2 weeks. We found that no precipitation was formed during this period, indicating that TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs possess excellent water dispersibility and are suitable for drug/gen delivery. Moreover, the TPE-E-PAMAM micelles can be modified to combine with the targeting moieties because of the presence of large numbers of amino groups at periphery, which can effectively deliver drug/gene to target location.

The UV−vis absorption spectra were conducted to understand the compositions of TPE-E-PAMAM. As shown in Fig. 3B, the TPE-E-PAMAM in water suspension have two maximum absorption peaks centered at 285 and 348nm, which can be assigned to the π–π* transition of the aromatic rings of TPE-E [64]. Besides, the absorption wavelength raised from 900nm, which indicates the formation of nanoparticles through self-assembly of TPE-E–PAMAM in pure aqueous solution. When illuminated with a UV lamp, the TPE-E-PAMAM suspension emits strong blue light presented as the inset of Fig. 3B (a, b), suggesting that the luminogen molecules are seriously aggregated in such aqueous media. The behavior may be ascribed to RIR mechanism of the chromophores [65-67]. The TPE dye was confined in the core of PAMAM dendrimers, and the intramolecular rotations are strikingly impeded. As a result, TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs display good water dispersibility and strong fluorescence emission simultaneously, making them promising for biological imaging applications.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of TPE-E-PAMAM were investigated in water by exploring the emission and stability of fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the maximum emission peak was located at 452nm while the excitation wavelength was broad from 263 to 333nm due to scattering of particles, which corresponded to UV−vis absorption. The broad excitation wavelength and narrow emission wavelength make TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs have merits of quantum dots [68]. More importantly, when the TPE-E-PAMAM suspension was irradiated by a UV lamp at 365nm for 1h, there was no significant change of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4B). The experiment validate that TPE-E-PAMAM micelles possess good photostability. Moreover, the quantum yield (QY) of TPE-E-PAMAM was examined using quinine sulfate as the reference dye. The QY of TPE-E-PAMAM is about 23.2% according to the examination. The strong and stable fluorescence as well as high QY makes the micelles potential fluorescent probes for biological imaging.

The cytotoxic effects of TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs against HeLa cells were determined by MTS, a colorimetric assay that measures the enzymatic activity of cellular mitochondria. The number of surviving cells is proportionate to the level of the formed formazan that can be determined by measuring absorbance at 570nm. As depicted in Fig. 5 , after incubation of HeLa cells with TPE-E-PAMAM for 24h, no significant difference on cell viability was observed. Furthermore, the optical image shows that cells were attached to the cell plates very well and still kept their normal morphology after cells were incubated with 40µg/mL–1 of TPE-E-PAMAM (Fig. 6 B). The optical microscopy observation also indicated that TPE-E-PAMAM possess good cytocompatibility.

The cellular imaging was studied using CLSM. HeLa cells were incubated with the TPE-E-PAMAM (40µg/mL) suspension for 3h at 37 °C in culture medium. As shown in Fig. 6A, strong blue fluorescence was observed mainly in the cytoplasm. Besides, no background interference was detected. Fig. 6C was the emerged image of A and B, which showed that most cells were labeled by TPE-E–PAMAM, indicating TPE-E–PAMAM LPNs can enter cells easily. The mechanism of TPE-E-PAMAM across cell membranes may be involved in surface properties, such as size and charge. TPE-E-PAMAM, with a large number of amino group outside of the shell, exhibit positive charge. The cationic TPE-E-PAMAM can perturb anionic cell membranes at physiological pH, which can be contributed to the action of paracellular and endocytic. It has been validated that PAMAM dendrimers are generally more easily transported across epithelial barriers than many conventional linear water-soluble polymers because of size and surface charge [69,70]. Therefore, considered their good biocompatibility and considerable endocytosis capability, TPE-E–PAMAM LPNs are promising candidates for biological applications.

In this study, we provide a simple strategy to develop highly emissive systems using an AIE-active chromophore and PAMAM dendrimers through covalent linkage between the peripheral amine of PAMAM dendrimers and the double bond of TPE-E. The strong inherent self-assembly tendency of PAMAM dendrimers owing to the effective intermolecular interactions makes TPE-E-PAMAM form compact sphere in water. The formation of TPE-E-PAMAM LPNs exhibited strong fluorescence, excellent water dispersion, stable dynamics, desirable cells uptake behavior and low cytotoxicity, which make them possible to be used for biosensing, imaging and drug/gene delivery. Besides, the unique three dimensional shapes of PAMAM dendrimers make TPE-E-PAMAM hold great potential as drug delivery agents. More importantly, large numbers of functional amino groups outside of the shell are amenable to various chemistry modification tools for drug and targeting agent conjugation. The combination of TPE derivatives and PAMAM dendrimers take better use of their advantages, which show potential applications in nanomedicine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022), and the National 973 Project (Nos. 2011CB935700).

Self-assembly of soft nanoparticles with tunable patchiness
